---
title: "Show me!"
subtitle: "Unit 4 Task 3"
author: "Sydney Ahlstrom Sanders"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn import metrics
# add the additional libraries you need to import for ML here
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
import pandas as pd
LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
# import your data here using polars and the URL
url = "https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4dwellings/raw/master/data-raw/dwellings_ml/dwellings_ml.csv"
dwellings = pl.read_csv(url)

print(dwellings.head())
```

## QUESTION 1

1. __Repeat the classification exercise using 2 different algorithms/models. Explain the differences between the 3 models__ (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task).

_I compared a Decision Tree, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting model. The Decision Tree is the simplest baseline and decides things using a single flowchart type of logic, it had the lowest overall accuracy at 90%. The Random Forest improves on this by making a "forest" of decision trees that vote on the final prediciton, reaching 92% accuracy. Gradient Boosting Models build trees sequentially, with each tree trying to correct the errors of the previous ones, often leading to improved performance (almost 93% accuracy in this case)._

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
dwellings = dwellings.with_columns(
    before1980 = pl.when(pl.col("yrbuilt") < 1980).then(1).otherwise(0)
)

X = dwellings.drop(["before1980", "before_1980", "yrbuilt", "parcel"], strict=False)
y = dwellings["before1980"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X.to_pandas(), y.to_pandas(), test_size=0.20, random_state=42
)


models = {
    "Decision Tree": DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=10, random_state=42),
    "Random Forest": RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=15, random_state=42),
    "Gradient Boosting": GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=5, random_state=42)
}


for name, model in models.items():
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
    acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    print(f"{name} Accuracy: {acc:.2%}")
```


## QUESTION 2

1. For each model (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task), display the classification report and the permutation importances for the most important features.  Discuss which model you would recommend to the Client and why.

_The Gradient Boosting model performed the best with the highest accuracy (93%), followed by the Random Forest and then the Decision Tree. I would recommend the Gradient Boosting model to the Client because it provides the most accurate predictions for identifying pre-1980 versus post-1980 housing styles._

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
import polars.selectors as cs

def get_importance_plot(model, name):
    result = permutation_importance(model, X_test.iloc[:500], y_test.iloc[:500], n_repeats=5, random_state=42)
    
    perm_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'feature': X_test.columns,
        'importance': result.importances_mean
    }).sort_values('importance', ascending=False).head(10)
    
    return ggplot(perm_df, aes(x='importance', y='feature', fill='importance')) + \
           geom_bar(stat='identity') + \
           labs(title=f"Permutation Importance: {name}")


for name, model in models.items():
    print(f"\n" + "="*30)
    print(f"REPORT FOR: {name}")
    print("="*30)
    
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
    print(metrics.classification_report(y_test, y_pred))

    p = get_importance_plot(model, name)
    p.show()
```
